As you can see, EPS is less invested in tech companies than VOO, and as most of the market gains of the current market are driven by tech innovation, the performance of EPS is mechanically lagging. These companies are mostly growth companies, and their performance is driven by future growth expectations, not necessarily by the current earnings.

What could be interesting is that the fund is providing investors with a larger exposure to financial companies. Lately, this has been a winning bet, as large-cap financials have benefited from important margins due to higher interest rates, an opposite trend from smaller companies that suffered because of the lower quality of their balance sheets. Right now, however, the outlook for financial companies is uncertain, mainly driven by interest rate dynamics, AI transformations, potential economic slowdowns, and regulations. Lower interest rates will compress margins but also stimulate the demand for credit; it is hard to actually predict how financial equities are going to behave. For now, interest rates are still at a high level, but the fact that the portfolio of EPS is rebalanced annually worries me, as I don’t think that the positioning of the fund will adapt fast enough to the changes of the industry. Investing in financial companies with a backward-looking earnings approach is not a good thing for me; it should be done about interest rate projections.

Other Considerations

In terms of income, as it focuses on earnings, EPS is doing slightly better than the S&P 500. The fund is distributing a dividend yield of 1.27%, backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 1.30% against 1.12% for VOO. This is a small income premium, not enough to shift the case.

EPS is not a new fund. Its inception date is 02/23/2007, and by then, the fund had only accumulated AUM of $1.3 Bn, less than 1000 times less than VOO. This goes to say that the market is not giving it much attention, and I agree with the investor’s sentiment. Over the last year, the fund saw net inflows of +110 Mn, reflecting a small continued appetite.

The fund’s average daily trading volume is $3.8 Mn (0.30% of the fund), which is small and results in a median bid/ask spread of 0.06%. This spread, because of the low daily trading volume of the fund, could widen disproportionately during events of market stress, making the fund’s liquidity at risk. This is also a key concern when in front of that you have the market leader VOO and its median bid/ask spread of 0.00%.

EPS comes with increased liquidity risk, concentration risk, underperformance risk, and costs, only to provide investors with better income and lower valuations. Not convincing.

Conclusion

A hold rating for EPS appears natural to me. The fund is not terrible in terms of performance but lacks a buy argument with regard to other alternatives in the market. The “smart beta” idea can be relevant, and I gave buy and strong buy ratings to such ETFs in the past, but I don’t think that the design of EPS will ever work to earn a long-time buy rating. EPS will never be a replacement for the S&P 500.

data.title: Niagen Bioscience: A Breakout Quarter That Now Faces Its Biggest Market Shift In Years
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I give Niagen Bioscience (NAGE) a hold rating because, although the company had a record quarter, it still faces an even more competitive landscape in the NAD+ market given the FDA's recent reversal on NMN. Tru Niagen sales still remain robust, and revenue growth is supported further by the company's expanding e-commerce, B2B distribution, and their rapidly growing footprint in the clinic space. The company also had record cash flow and has launched a share repurchase program to end their ten-year era of stock dilution. Nevertheless, it's still unclear as to whether the company's momentum in terms of revenue was durable or simply represents NMN's two-year absence from the marketplace. Either way, until post-NMN demand is clear and the impact of share repurchases becomes visible in the company's numbers, I believe the company merits a hold rating.

Earnings Recap

Niagen reported another record quarter due to three primary factors: strong double-digit growth, higher margins, and greater cash flow.

Revenue

Let's start with revenue. The company is up 33% YoY to almost $34 million—beating estimates by $2.9 million. This growth in net sales was driven by the company's strong momentum in their Tru Niagen product line, which itself is up an impressive 44% YoY to $26 million.

All this was supported by e-commerce sales of $19 million, up 29% YoY, and a 109% YoY surge in distribution from Watson's and new B2B partners totaling $7 million. Niagen's ingredient business remained steady at $6.9 million in comparison to last year's Q3 reported at $6.7 million, while pharmaceutical sales were down 44% YoY, reflecting limited demand in the company's early expansion of their distribution of Niagen Plus IVs and injectable therapies to over 1,000 clinics nationwide.

Other Metrics

Gross margins are up by one percentage point to 64.5% from last year's 63.5%, which was, in the words of CFO Ozan Pamir, "Driven by still some of the leftover inventory, the lower cost inventory we had, and also improved product mix." Sales and marketing expenses decreased to 25.8% of sales from last year's 27.5%, even though the company continued to invest in both e-commerce and clinical outreach. Meanwhile, operating income climbed to $4.2 million from $1.6 million in Q3 2024, and adjusted EBITDA more than doubled to $6.4 million up from last year's $3.5 million. The company's GAAP EPS also beat consensus by $0.04, and, of course, this is reflecting the company's higher revenue and improved profitability in comparison to last year.

Cash, Share Repurchases, and Guidance

The company ended the quarter with $64.3 million in cash and no debt, up from $60.5 million in Q2, after generating $12.8 million in cash from operations through the first nine months of 2025—already surpassing their FY 2024 $12.1 million generated in cash from operations. And, following the quarter, management launched a share repurchase program under which the company will repurchase up to $10 million of outstanding common stock over the span of about 24 months, and they also reaffirmed their full-year guidance for 25-30% YoY revenue growth.

My Analysis of Niagen's Earnings

To me, Niagen's Q3 results confirm everything I said in my previous article on the company: that this is a healthy, cash-generating machine; it's now generating revenue from places that didn't previously exist before, such as new B2B partnerships, a growing clinical network via its Niagen Plus IV and injectable therapies, and the development of a telehealth channel that will no doubt be a catalyst for FY 2026. CEO Robert Fried elaborated on this channel in the latest earnings call:

We are developing our own telehealth capability where one could go to truniagen.com or niagenplus.com and get a prescription from a physician online, much like the classic telehealth companies, and it would be delivered to their home via a pharmacy. But that -- we don't expect that functionality to be available for maybe two quarters probably, middle of next year.

And I don't want to understate the distribution business that grew 109% YoY with $7 million in revenue because it indicates that the company is having success leveraging its sales with third-party channels, which acts as a force multiplier now that the company is moving away from having to depend solely on its own website and marketing to now being able to rely on its distributors, clinics, and practitioners to drive their growth.

Meanwhile, their Tru Niagen product line that is up a whopping 44% YoY indicates that the product's demand is accelerating, which is extremely important given that the NAD+ market is both a crowded and a competitive category. And it's this NAD+ market that I want to touch on briefly before getting into the rest of my earnings analysis:

The NAD Market: The Reappearance of NMN

I explained in my last article that the goal of Tru Niagen as a dietary supplement is to boost NAD+ levels to what they were when NAD+ levels were at their peak in our early 20s, with NAD+ being essential for our cells to create the energy that powers our bodies and is at the forefront of aging and longevity science. There are two paths to increasing NAD+: one is through nicotinamide riboside [NR], which is what Tru Niagen is a patented form of, and then there's nicotinamide mononucleotide [NMN]. I don't want to get into the science behind their differences because it would be beyond the scope of this article.

But all the investor really needs to know is that back in November 2022, the FDA reclassified NMN as a drug ingredient and not as a dietary supplement, causing sellers such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) to ban the products from its marketplace as of March 13, 2023. This, in effect, gave Niagen Bioscience greater market share and was a boon to their revenue; I would know because I used to buy both Tru Niagen and NMN through Amazon, but after the ban I just continued to use Tru Niagen exclusively.

However, the FDA has just reversed its stance, and NMN is now lawful for use in dietary supplements. This reversal took place in late September, right around the end for the earnings period for Q3. Thus, the financial result of this reversal for the company won't appear until their next earnings report, which happens to be their annual report.

Nevertheless, the company did make several comments on this in their earnings call, with one of the major comments being that they believe the FDA's decision will face strong opposition, and, thus, the company expects further challenges to it. But when asked later by analyst Susan Anderson about how the NMN reversal affected the purchasing behavior by consumers of their own products, Robert Fried responded with the following statement:

It's only been 5 or 6 weeks, and we haven't noticed anything yet. ... there's a very good chance that the FDA will reverse this reversal again ... And as you know, we've tested many of the existing NMN products on the market and very few of them actually met label claims. Some of them had actually no NMN at all. We think NMN has in its purest form, an ability to elevate NAD, not as well as Niagen, obviously, but it still does it. It's still an effective way to elevate NAD. But at this point in time, we're not seeing any meaningful impact from the change in that rule.

Thus, though the company refrained from commenting as to how the decision will affect revenue, they don't seem fazed by the FDA's reversal at all. However, I think there are three ways to look at it:

The first is the bullish position, which is that the two-year NMN product ban has allowed the company to capture a greater market share, thus giving them a larger loyal customer base that won't bother needing to switch to NMN since they are already satisfied with Tru Niagen; this would explain much of the company's revenue boon as of late, and I, personally, believe this to be the case because I happen to be one of those consumers satisfied enough with Tru Niagen and feel no need to switch to NMN.

The second is the base position, which is the same as the above, except that revenue may not be able to sustain its current momentum given that consumers now have more options for increasing their NAD+ levels, thus warranting a more cautious approach to the company.

And the third is the bearish position, which is the belief that the company's revenue boon was directly caused by NMN's 2023 ban from the marketplace and, therefore, the FDA's reversal will stagnate or possibly even reverse the company's current revenue momentum given that consumers now have more options for increasing NAD+.

As stated above, we won't know for certain until we see the Q4 revenue numbers for October, November, and December, but it does warrant investors to be cautious given the uncertainty surrounding the FDA's decision.

Further Earnings Analysis

So now, beyond the company's revenue momentum and the latest NMN reversal, there are three other aspects of Q3 that stand out to me as being the most relevant for investors:

First, the company's cost structure is becoming one of its competitive assets, and we can see that with the company's sales & marketing that has improved to being 25.8% of revenue even though the company is expanding across e-commerce, distribution partnerships, and growing its network of clinics. This is on top of operating income tripling and adjusted EBITDA doubling, which indicates this main idea: profitability is rising faster than revenue as the business scales.

Second, though pharmaceutical-grade sales fell 44% YoY, making the segment not very meaningful as of today, it can be a catalyst that could change the company's profile going forward into 2026-2028. Much of Niagen Plus IV and injection therapies are suppressed by high clinic pricing. Here is Robert Fried in his own words:

One headwind that we've noticed so far is ... the price to consumers of getting these IVs at this point is quite high. ... We think that until those prices come down to more manageable levels, the volume is not going to reach its potential. So right now, it's not a huge business for us, this pharmaceutical ingredient business that's catering to Niagen Plus. And we don't think it's going to really take off in a significant way until those two things happen. Those two things being, number one, the injection market, particularly the telehealth market embraces it. And number two, the overall pricing at the clinics and physicians' offices comes down fairly dramatically.

So considering, in the latest earnings call, that management's estimate of there being as many as 10,000 potential clinic and physician locations where Niagen Plus or injection products can be offered to patients, in addition to the company's telehealth channel for patients to get their Niagen Plus prescriptions through an online doctor, all this indicates that the long-term opportunity for this segment is much larger than what the current revenue numbers show.

Third, I personally believe that, after looking at all these latest earnings figures, the strong cash flow is the greatest case to be made for an investment into the company. As I stated above for the earnings results, the company has generated more cash from operations in three quarters than it did for FY 2024—and that's on top of the company remaining debt-free. This is vital for a biotech company that needs strong cash flow for research and clinical expansion without needing to dilute the company further. And though the company recently announced its $10 million share repurchase program, this amount may not be large enough to move EPS, but it is a signal that management sees the stock as undervalued relative to the cash it currently generates.

Investor Takeaway

Q3 was impressive, there's no doubt about it: There was accelerating demand for Tru Niagen, rapidly expanding distribution, and more cash generation in three quarters than in any other prior year—all factors that give management more strategic flexibility. There are also multiple levers, such as e-commerce, B2B partners, a growing footprint in clinics nationwide, and an up-and-coming telehealth platform that could be a future catalyst for the company in the NAD+ space.

But while the company delivered a record quarter, I still think it has yet to be determined as to whether or not the company's achievements and momentum can be maintained in a post-NMN marketplace. And there is still the issue of stock dilution:

When I had published my last article on Niagen Bioscience back in September, I assigned the company a hold rating for one major reason: because although the company and their numbers looked great, Niagen's historic addiction to stock dilution had resulted in their shares outstanding increasing by 47.53% since 2016, with another 14.2% potential dilution from outstanding equity awards. These shares outstanding have now increased, though slightly, from their total of 79.75 million in Q2 to their now roughly 79.81 million in Q3, as seen in the YCharts below.

Because there were so many shares, I had to value the company using a DCF model and calculated their intrinsic value to be just $4.04; there were just too many slices of the company's pie to merit a higher valuation.

Now that the company has launched their share repurchase program, given their record cash generated from operations, it will hopefully mark the end of their ten-year stock dilution era. Though buying back $10 million of common stock may not make much of a difference to the company's intrinsic value, it's a start that investors should take note of.

In sum, the next two quarters will determine whether Niagen Bioscience's growth was a durable inflection point or a temporary lift from a competitive gap given by the FDA. And until the post-NMN marketplace trends are clear and the share repurchases become visible in the numbers, I believe a hold rating to be the most appropriate.
